questions.question: Why did the Mughals emphasize their Timurid ancestry over their Mongol (Genghis Khan) heritage?

options.A: Because Timur was a more recent ancestor.
options.B: Because Genghis Khan’s memory was associated with the massacre of innumerable people and the Uzbegs.
options.C: Because Timur had converted to Christianity.
options.D: Because the Mongols refused to recognize the Mughals as their descendants.

questions.explanation: The Mughals did not like to be called Mughal or Mongol because Genghis Khan's image was linked to massacres and was also linked to the Uzbegs, their Mongol competitors.

questions.question: What was the significance of the year 1504 in Babur's career?

options.A: He defeated Ibrahim Lodi.
options.B: He seized Kabul.
options.C: He lost Ferghana forever.
options.D: He built the Red Fort.

questions.explanation: After being forced to leave Ferghana by the Uzbegs, Babur wandered for years and finally seized Kabul in 1504.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major factor in Babur’s victory at the First Battle of Panipat?

options.A: Superior numbers of infantry.
options.B: Support from the Safavids of Iran.
options.C: Effective use of artillery and gunpowder.
options.D: The betrayal of Ibrahim Lodi by his own family.

questions.explanation: Babur used cannons and gunpowder effectively for the first time in 16th-century warfare in India during the Battle of Panipat.

questions.question: Humayun's reign was interrupted by the rise of which ruler?

options.A: Hemua
options.B: Sher Shah Suri
options.C: Rana Sanga
options.D: Malik Ambar

questions.explanation: Sher Khan (Sher Shah Suri) defeated Humayun twice, forcing him to flee to Iran and interrupted Mughal rule for 15 years.

questions.question: What did Akbar do to distance himself from the influence of his regent Bairam Khan in 1560?

options.A: He moved the capital to Lahore.
options.B: He declared himself independent and Bairam Khan was forced to go on a pilgrimage.
options.C: He started the Ibadat Khana.
options.D: He married Bairam Khan's daughter.

questions.explanation: In 1560, Akbar became independent of the regent Bairam Khan and other members of his domestic staff.

questions.question: The Mughal tradition of succession, where the inheritance was divided amongst all sons, is known as:

options.A: Primogeniture
options.B: Coparcenary inheritance
options.C: Sovereignty
options.D: Mansabdari

questions.explanation: The Mughals followed the Mughal and Timurid custom of coparcenary inheritance, or a division of the inheritance amongst all the sons.

questions.question: What happened when the Mughals became powerful regarding the 'submission' of other rulers?

options.A: All rulers were immediately executed.
options.B: Many rulers joined them voluntarily, like the Rajputs who married their daughters into Mughal families.
options.C: The Mughals paid other kings to serve them.
options.D: Other rulers ignored the Mughals completely.

questions.explanation: As the Mughals became powerful, many other rulers joined them voluntarily. Many Rajput families received high positions in return for marriage alliances.

questions.question: How did the Mughals treat defeated rulers like the Sisodiya Rajputs of Mewar?

options.A: They were treated with great respect and given their lands back as 'watan jagir'.
options.B: They were imprisoned for life.
options.C: Their lands were confiscated and given to the Uzbegs.
options.D: They were forced to convert to the king's religion.

questions.explanation: The Sisodiyas were defeated by Akbar but were treated honourably and given back their lands (watan) as assignments (watan jagir).

questions.question: What was the function of the 'Sawar' rank in the Mansabdari system?

options.A: It determined the official's personal salary.
options.B: It specified the number of horses and cavalrymen the official had to maintain.
options.C: It indicated the official's religious status.
options.D: It was the rank given only to the princes.

questions.explanation: The sawar rank indicated the number of cavalrymen (cavalry rank) a mansabdar was required to maintain.

questions.question: Why did most Mansabdars not actually reside in or administer their Jagirs?

options.A: They were not allowed to visit their lands.
options.B: They served in other parts of the empire and only had rights to the revenue collected by their servants.
options.C: They were afraid of the local peasants.
options.D: The Jagirs were located in foreign countries.

questions.explanation: Mansabdars did not reside in their jagirs; they only had rights to the revenue, while they themselves served in other parts of the empire.

questions.question: In Aurangzeb's reign, why did the 'Jagirdars' often try to extract as much revenue as possible?

options.A: Because the Sultan ordered them to do so.
options.B: Because there was a shortage of jagirs and they didn't know how long they would hold their current one.
options.C: To pay for the construction of temples.
options.D: To fund their own private wars against the Emperor.

questions.explanation: A huge increase in the number of mansabdars meant a long wait before they received a jagir. This led to jagirdars extracting maximum revenue while they had the chance.

questions.question: Why was the 'Zabt' system not possible in provinces like Gujarat and Bengal?

options.A: The people there refused to pay taxes.
options.B: These areas were too far for the Emperor to visit.
options.C: Detailed land surveys and careful record-keeping were not possible in those regions.
options.D: The land in those provinces was not suitable for crops.

questions.explanation: Zabt required careful survey of land and records, which was not possible in some provinces like Gujarat and Bengal.

questions.question: What role did 'Zamindars' play in the rural power structure during the Mughal era?

options.A: They were nomadic traders.
options.B: They were powerful local chieftains who sometimes rebelled against Mughal authority.
options.C: They were the official priests of the village.
options.D: They were the slaves of the Mansabdars.

questions.explanation: Zamindars were powerful local intermediaries. In some areas, they exercised great power and their rebellions challenged the stability of the empire.

questions.question: The 'Ain-i Akbari' provides detailed information about which of the following?

options.A: The history of the Delhi Sultanate.
options.B: Statistical details of crops, yields, prices, and wages.
options.C: The life of Genghis Khan.
options.D: Instructions on how to build a cannon.

questions.explanation: Ain-i Akbari is famous for its rich statistical details about various aspects of the empire including crops and prices.

questions.question: How did Akbar’s discussions in the 'Ibadat Khana' change his view on religion?

options.A: He decided to become a monk.
options.B: He realized that religious scholars who emphasized ritual and dogma were often bigots.
options.C: He concluded that only one religion was true.
options.D: He banned all religious discussions in his court.

questions.explanation: His interaction with different faiths led him to realize that dogmatic religious scholars often created divisions in society.

questions.question: The ethical system of 'Sulh-i kul' was focused on:

options.A: Conversion of all subjects to one faith.
options.B: Honesty, justice, and peace that was universally applicable.
options.C: Collecting more taxes from non-Muslims.
options.D: Promoting the worship of the Emperor as a God.

questions.explanation: Sulh-i kul was a system of ethics—honesty, justice, peace—that did not discriminate between people of different religions.

questions.question: Which of the following was a responsibility of the 'Faujdar' in a Suba?

options.A: Acting as a religious in-charge.
options.B: Maintaining the financial accounts.
options.C: Serving as a military commander.
options.D: Writing the history of the province.

questions.explanation: In the administrative structure, the Faujdars were military commanders who supported the Subadar.

questions.question: What does the term 'Dogma' mean in the context of the Mughal era religious discussions?

options.A: A type of prayer.
options.B: A statement or interpretation declared as authoritative with the expectation that it would be followed without question.
options.C: A tax on religious gatherings.
options.D: A person who changes their religion.

questions.explanation: Dogma refers to authoritative interpretations that were expected to be followed blindly, something Akbar questioned.

questions.question: How did Nur Jahan exercise her influence in the Mughal court?

options.A: By leading the army in battles.
options.B: By having coins struck in her name and managing royal seals.
options.C: By writing the biography of Jahangir.
options.D: By moving the capital to Kashmir.

questions.explanation: Jahangir struck silver coins bearing his titles on one side and the inscription 'struck in the name of the Queen Begum, Nur Jahan' on the other.

questions.question: Which Mughal Emperor abolished the 'Jizya' tax on non-Muslims, only for it to be reintroduced later?

options.A: Babur
options.B: Akbar
options.C: Shah Jahan
options.D: Aurangzeb

questions.explanation: Akbar abolished the tax, but it was reintroduced by Aurangzeb much later in the 17th century.

questions.question: Why were the 'Sikhs' and 'Marathas' mentioned as challenges in the 17th century?

options.A: They were foreign invaders.
options.B: They were internal groups that rebelled against Aurangzeb’s policies.
options.C: They were the primary authors of the Akbar Nama.
options.D: They were the main trade partners of the Mughals.

questions.explanation: Aurangzeb’s long wars and administrative policies led to resistance from several groups, including the Sikhs, Marathas, and Jats.

questions.question: In the late 17th century, why did the Mughal Empire face an 'agrarian crisis'?

options.A: Because the soil became infertile everywhere.
options.B: Excessive tax pressure on peasants by the state and the greed of Jagirdars.
options.C: The peasants forgot how to farm.
options.D: A lack of water in the Ganga and Yamuna rivers.

questions.explanation: High revenue demands and the unstable nature of jagirs led to the exploitation of peasants, causing widespread poverty and rebellion.
questions.source_section: The Mughal Empire in the 17th Century

questions.question: How many 'Great Mughals' (from Babur to Aurangzeb) are typically identified in this period of history?

options.A: Four
options.B: Six
options.C: Eight
options.D: Ten

questions.explanation: The line of major Mughal emperors includes Babur, Humayun, Akbar, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb.

questions.question: The city of Fatehpur Sikri is most closely associated with which ruler?

options.A: Babur
options.B: Akbar
options.C: Shah Jahan
options.D: Aurangzeb

questions.explanation: Akbar built his new capital at Fatehpur Sikri and held his famous religious debates there.

questions.question: What led to the eventual fragmentation of the Mughal Empire after the death of Aurangzeb?

options.A: The invasion of the British only.
options.B: The rise of powerful independent provinces under former Mughal governors.
options.C: The destruction of the Taj Mahal.
options.D: The discovery of a new continent.

questions.explanation: Governors of large provinces (subas) like Awadh, Bengal, and Hyderabad began to consolidate independent power as the central authority weakened.
questions.source_section: The Mughal Empire in the 17th Century
